clinicalStem: A 4-year-old boy is brought by his mother who noticed his face was puffy when he woke up over the past week. She also reports his urine looks frothy and his tummy seems bloated. He has been eating less than usual. On examination he weighs 18 kg (up from 15 kg two months ago), has periorbital and bilateral pitting pedal oedema, and a distended abdomen with shifting dullness. Blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows 4+ protein and no blood. Serum albumin is 14 g/L, total cholesterol 9.2 mmol/L, and urea and creatinine are normal. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: The classic nephrotic syndrome tetrad — massive proteinuria (4+ on dipstick), hypoalbuminaemia (14 g/L), oedema, and hypercholesterolaemia — in a 3–8 year-old boy strongly points to idiopathic nephrotic syndrome, most commonly minimal change disease. There is no haematuria, no hypertension, and renal function is preserved, which distinguishes it from glomerulonephritis. Minimal change disease accounts for approximately 90% of nephrotic syndrome in this age group.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis presents with haematuria (smoky urine), hypertension, and oedema, with proteinuria usually only 1–2+; it does not cause the massive hypoalbuminaemia or hypercholesterolaemia seen here.

Idiopathic nephrotic syndrome (minimal change disease) is correct: 4-year-old boy, periorbital oedema, massive proteinuria without haematuria, hypoalbuminaemia, hypercholesterolaemia, and normal renal function are the defining features.
HSP nephritis typically follows a purpuric rash over the buttocks and lower limbs, joint pain, and abdominal pain; the urinalysis shows haematuria as well as proteinuria.
Congestive cardiac failure causes oedema but the JVP is raised, hepatomegaly is present, and there is no massive proteinuria or hypoalbuminaemia of this degree.
Protein-losing enteropathy causes hypoalbuminaemia and oedema but is accompanied by gastrointestinal symptoms and diarrhoea; urinary protein is not elevated.
clinicalPearl: In a child aged 1–8 years, the nephrotic syndrome tetrad (oedema, massive proteinuria, hypoalbuminaemia, hypercholesterolaemia) without haematuria or hypertension is minimal change disease until proven otherwise.
keyClue: 4-year-old boy with periorbital puffiness, 4+ proteinuria on dipstick, serum albumin 14 g/L, and hypercholesterolaemia with no haematuria.
commonExamTrap: Students confuse nephrotic with nephritic syndrome; the key discriminators are massive proteinuria + hypoalbuminaemia (nephrotic) vs haematuria + hypertension + renal impairment (nephritic).
missedConcept: The classic clinical and biochemical tetrad of nephrotic syndrome and its predominant cause in young children.

clinicalStem: A 5-year-old girl is newly diagnosed with idiopathic nephrotic syndrome. She has periorbital and pedal oedema, urine protein:creatinine ratio of 550 mg/mmol (normal <20), serum albumin 12 g/L, and total cholesterol 10.1 mmol/L. Renal function is normal, blood pressure is 88/55 mmHg, and urine shows no red cells or casts. She has received all recommended vaccines. There is no evidence of infection. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: Oral prednisolone 60 mg/m² per day (max 60 mg) for 4–6 weeks
Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor alone for 3 months
explanation: ISKDC and IPNA guidelines recommend oral prednisolone 60 mg/m²/day (maximum 60 mg daily) for 4–6 weeks as first-line therapy for a first episode of idiopathic nephrotic syndrome in children. This induces remission in approximately 90% of steroid-sensitive cases (predominantly minimal change disease). IV methylprednisolone is reserved for severe or relapsing cases; cyclosporin and mycophenolate are second-line agents for steroid-dependent or frequent-relapsing disease.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Intravenous methylprednisolone is reserved for severe oedema preventing oral intake, very frequent relapses, or when IV access is required; oral prednisolone is standard for a first episode.
Oral prednisolone 60 mg/m²/day for 4–6 weeks is correct per ISKDC/IPNA protocol for first-episode idiopathic nephrotic syndrome in children.
Cyclosporin A is a second-line steroid-sparing agent used for steroid-dependent or frequently relapsing nephrotic syndrome, not as initial therapy.
Mycophenolate mofetil is also a second-line agent for steroid-dependent disease and is not appropriate as first-line treatment for a new presentation.
ACE inhibitors reduce proteinuria and are used adjunctively or in steroid-resistant disease but cannot induce remission of minimal change disease alone.
clinicalPearl: First-episode idiopathic nephrotic syndrome in children is treated with oral prednisolone 60 mg/m²/day (max 60 mg) for 4 weeks, then 40 mg/m² on alternate days for 4 weeks; remission is defined as urine protein trace or negative for 3 consecutive days.
keyClue: Newly diagnosed idiopathic nephrotic syndrome in a 5-year-old with normal renal function and no haematuria — first-line therapy question.
commonExamTrap: Jumping to IV methylprednisolone or cyclosporin without recognising that oral prednisolone is standard first-line therapy for a first episode.
missedConcept: ISKDC protocol for first-episode nephrotic syndrome: oral prednisolone duration and dose.

clinicalStem: A 7-year-old boy with a 2-year history of steroid-sensitive nephrotic syndrome currently in relapse presents with a 12-hour history of diffuse abdominal pain, fever of 38.8°C, and vomiting. His abdomen is tender with guarding particularly in the right lower quadrant and periumbilical region. Urine dipstick shows 4+ protein. His mother reports he has been taking his prednisolone course. White cell count is 18 × 10⁹/L with 85% neutrophils. Serum albumin is 10 g/L. Which complication of nephrotic syndrome does this presentation most likely represent?
explanation: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) is a recognised and serious complication of nephrotic syndrome in children. Hypoalbuminaemia impairs opsonisation and immunoglobulin levels are low, predisposing to infection with encapsulated organisms (Streptococcus pneumoniae most common, followed by Gram-negatives). The combination of active nephrotic syndrome (4+ protein, albumin 10 g/L), fever, abdominal pain, guarding, and leukocytosis in a child on prednisolone should prompt urgent diagnostic peritoneal aspiration and empirical antibiotics. Acute appendicitis is possible but SBP is a specific and life-threatening complication to consider first in this context.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Acute appendicitis is possible but the specific context of active nephrotic syndrome with its known susceptibility to SBP, combined with diffuse tenderness (not isolated RIF), makes SBP the priority diagnosis requiring immediate ascitic tap and antibiotics.

Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis is correct: low serum albumin causing impaired immunity, ascites as a culture medium, steroid-induced immunosuppression, and fever with abdominal guarding in a child with active nephrotic syndrome are the defining features.

Thromboembolism is a recognised complication (renal vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism) but mesenteric ischaemia would present with bloody diarrhoea and is far less common than SBP in this clinical context.
Steroid-induced peptic ulceration can occur but presentation with peritonitis from perforation in a child on standard prednisolone doses for nephrotic syndrome is uncommon compared with SBP.
Viral gastroenteritis does not typically produce the degree of peritonism with guarding and a markedly raised neutrophil-predominant leucocytosis seen here.
clinicalPearl: In a nephrotic child with fever and abdominal pain, always think spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (Streptococcus pneumoniae most common); the ascites is the culture medium and hypoalbuminaemia impairs innate immunity.
keyClue: Active nephrotic syndrome with hypoalbuminaemia + fever + abdominal guarding + neutrophilia — high suspicion for spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.
commonExamTrap: Misdiagnosing SBP as acute appendicitis and going directly to surgery without considering the nephrotic context and performing an ascitic tap first.
missedConcept: Impaired immunity in nephrotic syndrome (low IgG, low complement) predisposes to SBP from encapsulated organisms, particularly Streptococcus pneumoniae.
redFlagReason: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in a child with nephrotic syndrome is life-threatening; delay in diagnosis and empirical antibiotics can lead to septic shock and death.

clinicalStem: A 3-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department with a 5-day history of bloody diarrhoea following a barbecue where she ate undercooked beef burgers. Over the past 2 days she has become increasingly irritable, pale, and oliguric. On examination she appears unwell with conjunctival pallor, petechiae over her trunk and legs, a blood pressure of 105/70 mmHg, and facial puffiness. Investigations show: Hb 62 g/L (↓), platelets 35 × 10⁹/L (↓), WBC 14 × 10⁹/L, creatinine 280 µmol/L (↑), LDH 1800 U/L (↑), and blood film with fragmented red cells. Urinalysis shows haematuria and proteinuria. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura with coincidental renal impairment
Disseminated intravascular coagulation secondary to sepsis
explanation: The triad of microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (fragmented RBCs on film, raised LDH), thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury (oliguria, raised creatinine) following a prodrome of bloody diarrhoea due to Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (STEC) in a young child is the classic presentation of haemolytic uraemic syndrome (HUS). It is the most common cause of AKI in children under 5 years. The bloody diarrhoea after consuming undercooked meat points to STEC O157:H7 as the trigger.
wrongAnswerExplanations: ITP causes isolated thrombocytopenia with petechiae but does not cause microangiopathic haemolysis (fragmented RBCs, raised LDH) or acute kidney injury to this degree.
Haemolytic uraemic syndrome is correct: the triad of MAHA, thrombocytopenia, and AKI following bloody diarrhoea (STEC) is the textbook presentation of HUS in a young child.

DIC causes thrombocytopenia and a coagulopathy (prolonged PT/APTT, low fibrinogen) in the context of overwhelming sepsis; while renal impairment can occur, the specific sequence of bloody diarrhoea followed by this triad is characteristic of HUS.
Post-streptococcal GN presents with haematuria, hypertension, and oedema 1–3 weeks after throat or skin infection; it does not cause fragmented red cells on film or severe thrombocytopenia.

TTP classically affects adults, prominently features neurological involvement and fever, and is less commonly triggered by Shiga toxin; in a 3-year-old with post-diarrhoeal presentation, HUS is far more likely.
clinicalPearl: HUS = bloody diarrhoea (STEC O157:H7) + microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia + thrombocytopenia + AKI in a child under 5; avoid antibiotics as they may increase Shiga toxin release.
keyClue: 3-year-old with bloody diarrhoea after undercooked meat, then pallor, oliguria, fragmented RBCs, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury.
commonExamTrap: Selecting DIC because thrombocytopenia and organ dysfunction are present; DIC requires coagulopathy (PT/APTT prolongation, low fibrinogen) and the classic post-diarrhoeal sequence strongly favours HUS.
missedConcept: The classic triad of HUS and its association with Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157:H7 following bloody diarrhoea in young children.
redFlagReason: HUS causes life-threatening AKI, severe anaemia, and thrombocytopenia; the child requires urgent assessment for dialysis, packed RBC transfusion, and ITU-level monitoring.

clinicalStem: A 6-year-old boy with haemolytic uraemic syndrome is admitted with anuria for 18 hours. His serum potassium is 7.2 mmol/L and is rising. The ECG shows peaked T-waves and a widened QRS complex. His blood pressure is 130/88 mmHg and he has periorbital oedema. Serum bicarbonate is 12 mmol/L. Which is the single most urgent next step in his management?
options: Urgent renal ultrasound to exclude obstructive uropathy
IV calcium gluconate 10% 0.5 mL/kg over 5–10 minutes
Sodium bicarbonate infusion 1–2 mmol/kg IV over 30 minutes
Salbutamol nebuliser 2.5 mg to drive potassium intracellularly
explanation: ECG changes (peaked T-waves and widened QRS) in the setting of hyperkalaemia (7.2 mmol/L) indicate life-threatening cardiac toxicity. The immediate priority following ABCDE principles is cardiac membrane stabilisation with intravenous calcium gluconate, which acts within 1–3 minutes to reduce the risk of ventricular fibrillation. Calcium does not lower the serum potassium level but protects the myocardium while other measures (bicarbonate, salbutamol, dialysis) are arranged. It must be given first.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Renal ultrasound is indicated to exclude post-renal AKI but is not the immediate priority when the child has ECG evidence of life-threatening hyperkalaemia requiring cardiac stabilisation.
IV calcium gluconate is correct as the immediate first step: ECG changes (peaked T-waves, widened QRS) signal cardiac membrane instability that must be stabilised before other measures.

Sodium bicarbonate shifts potassium intracellularly and is an important second step, but it does not protect the myocardium from immediate arrhythmia — calcium must come first when ECG changes are present.
Salbutamol nebulisation also drives potassium into cells but acts over 15–30 minutes and does not provide immediate cardiac membrane protection; it is a useful second-line measure after calcium.
Dialysis is the definitive treatment for hyperkalaemia in anuric HUS, but set-up takes time; calcium gluconate must be given immediately to prevent fatal arrhythmia while dialysis is arranged.
clinicalPearl: In hyperkalaemia with ECG changes (peaked T-waves, widened QRS, sine wave), give IV calcium gluconate FIRST to stabilise the myocardium — it does not lower K⁺ but buys time; cardiac arrest can occur within minutes.
keyClue: Serum K⁺ 7.2 mmol/L WITH peaked T-waves and widened QRS on ECG — imminent arrhythmia, cardiac membrane stabilisation is the single most urgent step.
commonExamTrap: Choosing sodium bicarbonate or dialysis as the first step; both are important but calcium gluconate must precede all other measures when ECG changes indicate myocardial toxicity.
missedConcept: The hierarchy of hyperkalaemia management: calcium gluconate (stabilise membrane) → then measures to shift K⁺ in (bicarbonate, salbutamol, insulin-dextrose) → then remove K⁺ (dialysis, resonium).
redFlagReason: ECG changes from hyperkalaemia (peaked T-waves, widened QRS) indicate imminent ventricular fibrillation and cardiac arrest; immediate IV calcium gluconate is a life-saving intervention.

clinicalStem: A 9-month-old male infant is brought to the emergency department by his parents who report intermittent episodes of sudden, severe crying with leg-drawing over the past 8 hours. Between episodes he appears lethargic and pale. He vomited twice and has not passed a stool. On examination he is afebrile, HR 145 bpm, RR 30, weight 9 kg. There is a vague sausage-shaped mass palpable in the right upper quadrant. His nappy contains some dark red, jelly-like material. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Meckel's diverticulum with painless rectal bleeding
explanation: Intussusception is the most common cause of intestinal obstruction in infants between 5 months and 2 years. The classic triad is intermittent colicky pain with leg-drawing, a sausage-shaped right upper quadrant mass, and redcurrant jelly (mucus and blood) in the stool. The intermittent nature with pallor and lethargy between episodes and the pathognomonic stool appearance make intussusception the diagnosis. Urgent reduction (hydrostatic or pneumatic enema) is required.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Acute gastroenteritis causes vomiting and diarrhoea but the stool is watery/green, not dark red jelly-like; a palpable abdominal mass and the characteristic intermittent colicky episodes with lethargy between attacks are not features of gastroenteritis.

Meckel's diverticulum typically causes painless bright rectal bleeding in a toddler due to ectopic gastric mucosa; it does not cause the colicky pain pattern, a palpable abdominal mass, or redcurrant jelly stool.
Intussusception is correct: the classic triad of intermittent colicky pain with leg-drawing, sausage-shaped RUQ mass, and redcurrant jelly stool in a 9-month-old is the textbook presentation.
Incarcerated inguinal hernia presents with a tender, irreducible groin lump with signs of obstruction; it does not produce a RUQ mass or redcurrant jelly stool.

Hirschsprung's enterocolitis presents in neonates with failure to pass meconium and later with abdominal distension and explosive diarrhoea; it does not cause the intermittent colicky pattern or redcurrant jelly stool.
clinicalPearl: Intussusception classic triad: intermittent colicky pain (drawing up legs), sausage-shaped RUQ mass, and redcurrant jelly stool — in an infant aged 5 months to 2 years; confirm with USS and treat with pneumatic/hydrostatic reduction.
keyClue: 9-month-old boy with intermittent colicky crying, a sausage-shaped RUQ mass, and dark red jelly-like (redcurrant jelly) stool.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing acute gastroenteritis and discharging the infant; the lethargy between attacks, the mass, and the characteristic bloody stool appearance should not be missed.
missedConcept: The classic triad of intussusception and the age group at highest risk (5 months to 2 years, peak 6–12 months).
redFlagReason: Intussusception is a surgical emergency; delayed reduction leads to bowel ischaemia, perforation, and peritonitis — rapid diagnosis and treatment are essential.

clinicalStem: A 4-week-old male infant is brought by his mother with a 10-day history of progressive vomiting after feeds. She describes the vomiting as very forceful — 'it shoots across the room' — and states it is non-bilious. The baby is hungry and wants to feed immediately after vomiting. He has lost 400 g since his birth weight check at 2 weeks. On examination he is dehydrated with a sunken fontanelle. During a test feed, visible gastric peristalsis is observed and an olive-shaped mass is palpable in the right epigastrium. Serum electrolytes show Na⁺ 132 mmol/L, K⁺ 3.0 mmol/L, Cl⁻ 88 mmol/L, bicarbonate 32 mmol/L. Which is the single best next investigation?
explanation: Infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis (IHPS) presents in the first 2–8 weeks of life (peak 3–6 weeks) with projectile non-bilious vomiting in a hungry infant, visible gastric peristalsis, and a palpable pyloric olive. The hypochloraemic, hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis is the classic electrolyte picture. The investigation of choice is abdominal ultrasound, which demonstrates a thickened pyloric muscle (length ≥16 mm, wall thickness ≥4 mm) and is both sensitive and specific without radiation. Definitive treatment after electrolyte correction is Ramstedt pyloromyotomy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Barium meal was the historical gold standard but is now largely replaced by ultrasound due to the absence of radiation, greater availability, and excellent sensitivity; it is not the current first-line investigation.
Upper GI endoscopy is not appropriate for initial investigation of pyloric stenosis in a neonate; it does not visualise the pyloric muscle thickness and carries procedural risks.
Abdominal ultrasound is correct: it is non-invasive, non-irradiating, and accurately measures pyloric muscle thickness and length, with sensitivity and specificity >95% for IHPS.
Serum gastrin is not a standard investigation for pyloric stenosis; hypergastrinaemia can occur in IHPS but is neither sensitive nor specific and does not confirm the diagnosis.
Plain abdominal X-ray may show a distended stomach but is non-specific and cannot diagnose pyloric stenosis or measure pyloric dimensions.
clinicalPearl: Pyloric stenosis = 3–6 week male infant, projectile non-bilious vomiting, hungry after vomiting, hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis; investigate with ultrasound (pyloric length ≥16 mm, wall thickness ≥4 mm); treat with Ramstedt pyloromyotomy after correction of electrolytes.
keyClue: 4-week-old male, projectile non-bilious vomiting, hungry after feeds, olive-shaped epigastric mass, hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis.
commonExamTrap: Selecting barium meal instead of ultrasound; USS is the current gold standard for diagnosis and avoids radiation in a neonate.
missedConcept: The classic electrolyte abnormality of pyloric stenosis is hypochloraemic, hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis (loss of HCl in vomit); this must be corrected before surgery.

clinicalStem: A 6-week-old male infant is seen in the outpatient clinic for constipation. His mother reports he has never had a spontaneous bowel movement; stools are only passed with rectal stimulation or suppositories. He was born at term after an uncomplicated delivery and passed meconium at 48 hours of age. He has a distended abdomen and on digital rectal examination there is an explosive gush of stool and flatus on withdrawal of the finger. His birth weight was 3.4 kg; current weight is 3.6 kg. Which investigation would best confirm the likely diagnosis?
options: Abdominal X-ray showing dilated bowel loops
Barium enema showing a transition zone
Full thickness rectal biopsy demonstrating absence of ganglion cells
Anorectal manometry showing failure of internal sphincter relaxation
explanation: Hirschsprung's disease (HD) is characterised by the absence of ganglion cells in the myenteric and submucosal plexuses of the distal bowel. The gold standard for diagnosis is full-thickness rectal biopsy demonstrating the absence of ganglion cells (aganglionosis) and the presence of hypertrophied nerve trunks. Delayed passage of meconium (>48 hours), failure to pass stools spontaneously, abdominal distension, and the explosive decompression on rectal examination in a neonate/young infant are classic features. Barium enema showing a transition zone and anorectal manometry are useful but less definitive than histology.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Abdominal X-ray showing dilated loops supports the diagnosis of obstruction but is non-specific and cannot confirm aganglionosis; it does not replace histological confirmation.
Barium enema showing a transition zone is suggestive and useful for planning surgery, but it can be normal in total colonic HD or in neonates; it is not the definitive gold standard.
Full-thickness rectal biopsy demonstrating absent ganglion cells is the gold standard diagnostic test for Hirschsprung's disease and is correct.

Anorectal manometry showing absent rectoanal inhibitory reflex (RAIR) is supportive and is used as a screening test in older children, but it cannot be reliably performed in neonates and is not the confirmatory gold standard.
Colonoscopy is not appropriate in a 6-week-old infant with suspected Hirschsprung's disease; it does not provide the transmural histological assessment needed.
clinicalPearl: Hirschsprung's disease should be suspected when a neonate fails to pass meconium within 48 hours or has never had a spontaneous stool; gold standard diagnosis is full-thickness rectal biopsy showing absent ganglion cells.
keyClue: Never had a spontaneous bowel movement, delayed meconium, explosive decompression on rectal examination in a 6-week-old — suspect Hirschsprung's disease.
commonExamTrap: Choosing barium enema as the gold standard; while it shows the transition zone, full-thickness biopsy with histology is the definitive confirmatory test.
missedConcept: The absence of ganglion cells (aganglionosis) in the myenteric plexus is the pathological basis of Hirschsprung's disease; full-thickness biopsy is required for definitive diagnosis.

clinicalStem: A 6-week-old female infant is reviewed in the paediatric cardiology clinic. She was noted to have a murmur at her newborn check. Her mother reports that feeds take longer than expected and she sweats profusely during feeding. Weight is on the 9th centile (birth weight was on the 50th centile). On examination she is pink with mild tachypnoea (RR 58) and intercostal recession. There is a harsh pansystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border, loudest at grade 4/6. The liver is palpable 3 cm below the costal margin. Chest X-ray shows cardiomegaly with increased pulmonary vascular markings. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Ventricular septal defect with left-to-right shunt causing heart failure
explanation: Ventricular septal defect (VSD) is the most common congenital heart defect. A large VSD causes a left-to-right shunt, leading to pulmonary overcirculation and eventually cardiac failure in infancy. Clinical features include a harsh pansystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border, feeding difficulty, sweating, poor weight gain, tachypnoea, hepatomegaly, and cardiomegaly with increased pulmonary vascular markings on CXR. The infant is pink because this is a left-to-right shunt without cyanosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pulmonary stenosis produces an ejection systolic murmur at the upper left sternal border with a normal or reduced pulmonary vascular pattern; it does not cause the signs of pulmonary overcirculation seen here.
Tetralogy of Fallot causes cyanosis due to right-to-left shunting, produces a boot-shaped heart with decreased pulmonary vascular markings on CXR — the opposite of this presentation.

Ventricular septal defect is correct: large VSD with left-to-right shunt causes the characteristic harsh pansystolic murmur at LLSB, feeding difficulty, sweating, poor weight gain, hepatomegaly, cardiomegaly, and increased pulmonary markings.
Patent ductus arteriosus produces a continuous machinery murmur at the upper left sternal border/subclavicular region, not a pansystolic murmur at the LLSB.
Transposition of the great arteries presents in the first hours of life with severe cyanosis unresponsive to oxygen and requires urgent prostaglandin infusion; the infant here is pink.
clinicalPearl: Large VSD: pansystolic murmur at LLSB, feeding difficulty and sweating (high metabolic demand), poor weight gain, and a pink infant with cardiomegaly and increased pulmonary markings — left-to-right shunt causing cardiac failure.
keyClue: Pink infant, harsh pansystolic murmur at left lower sternal border, cardiomegaly, increased pulmonary markings, signs of cardiac failure (hepatomegaly, poor weight gain).
commonExamTrap: Selecting TOF because the child has a murmur and cardiac symptoms; TOF causes cyanosis and decreased pulmonary markings — the opposite picture to VSD.
missedConcept: Left-to-right shunts (VSD, ASD, PDA) cause pulmonary overcirculation and heart failure but NOT cyanosis; right-to-left shunts (TOF, TGA) cause cyanosis.

clinicalStem: An 8-month-old male infant with known Tetralogy of Fallot becomes acutely deeply cyanosed and hypoxic during the morning examination by his medical student. The infant is agitated, crying, and his oxygen saturations drop from a baseline of 80% to 55%. The murmur, which was previously loud, is now barely audible. He has been awaiting surgical repair. Which is the most appropriate immediate management for this hypercyanotic (tet) spell?
options: Administer IV digoxin 10 mcg/kg and increase oxygen by face mask
Place in knee-chest position, give high-flow oxygen, and administer IV morphine 0.1 mg/kg
Urgent needle decompression for suspected pneumothorax
Adenosine 0.1 mg/kg IV rapid bolus for probable SVT
explanation: A hypercyanotic (tet) spell occurs when infundibular spasm or reduced systemic vascular resistance increases right-to-left shunting across the VSD, causing profound cyanosis. The disappearance of the murmur reflects reduced pulmonary blood flow. The knee-chest position (or squatting in older children) increases systemic vascular resistance, reducing right-to-left shunting and improving pulmonary blood flow. High-flow oxygen is given to reduce hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, and IV morphine (or oral/IM) reduces infundibular spasm, agitation, and the sympathetic drive. Beta-blockers (propranolol IV), phenylephrine, and IV bicarbonate are additional measures. This is a paediatric emergency.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Digoxin is not indicated for tet spells; it increases contractility which may worsen infundibular spasm, and is not part of the acute management algorithm.

Knee-chest position plus high-flow oxygen and IV morphine is correct: this is the standard initial management of a hypercyanotic spell to increase SVR, reduce infundibular spasm, and improve pulmonary blood flow.
Needle decompression is for tension pneumothorax; there is no clinical history of trauma, respiratory asymmetry, or tracheal deviation to suggest this diagnosis.
Adenosine is for SVT, which presents with a very rapid regular narrow-complex tachycardia; this infant has a known structural heart defect and the clinical picture is of a tet spell, not SVT.
DC cardioversion is for haemodynamically unstable tachyarrhythmias; this infant has a tet spell, not an arrhythmia.
clinicalPearl: In a tet spell: place in knee-chest position (raises SVR, reduces R→L shunt), give high-flow O₂, IV morphine to reduce infundibular spasm and agitation; a disappearing murmur with worsening cyanosis = less blood reaching the lungs.
keyClue: Known TOF, acute worsening cyanosis, murmur that was loud is now barely audible — this is a hypercyanotic (tet) spell from infundibular spasm.
commonExamTrap: Attempting to give digoxin (worsens spasm) or missing the significance of the disappearing murmur (reduced pulmonary flow, not reduced defect).
missedConcept: In a tet spell, the murmur diminishes because pulmonary blood flow decreases (not because the VSD has closed); management targets SVR increase and infundibular relaxation.
redFlagReason: A hypercyanotic tet spell is a life-threatening emergency; oxygen saturations of 55% can cause brain injury and cardiac arrest within minutes if untreated.

clinicalStem: A 20-month-old boy is brought to clinic by his parents who are concerned he is not talking. He was born at term with no antenatal or perinatal complications. He sat unsupported at 8 months, walked at 14 months, and has 5 words. He does not yet point to objects, does not respond to his name consistently, makes limited eye contact, and does not wave goodbye. He plays repetitively with spinning wheels on toy cars. His hearing screen at birth was normal. Examination is otherwise unremarkable. Growth parameters are all on the 50th centile. Which investigation should be prioritised first?
options: Karyotyping and FISH for fragile X syndrome
Thyroid function tests and lead level
explanation: Any child presenting with speech/language delay should have formal audiological assessment as the first-line investigation, as undetected hearing loss is the most common and treatable cause of language delay. Although the newborn hearing screen was normal, it does not exclude later acquired or progressive hearing loss. In this child, however, the constellation of features — no pointing, not responding to name, limited eye contact, restricted social interaction, and repetitive play — strongly suggests autism spectrum disorder (ASD). Formal hearing assessment is still mandatory before proceeding with developmental assessment to rule out a reversible cause.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Karyotyping and fragile X testing are important when there is intellectual disability, dysmorphic features, or a family history, but formal hearing assessment should precede genetic evaluation in a child with speech delay.
MRI brain is indicated when there are neurological signs, regression, asymmetry, or macrocephaly/microcephaly; it is not the first-line investigation for developmental delay without such features.
Formal hearing assessment is correct: it must be performed in every child with speech or language delay before any other investigation, as hearing loss is the most common reversible cause.
Thyroid function tests and lead levels are part of a broader metabolic screen for developmental delay but are lower priority than excluding hearing impairment, which is directly addressable.

EEG is indicated when there is clinical suspicion of seizures, Landau-Kleffner syndrome, or regression with behavioural change; it is not the first-line investigation for language delay with autistic features.
clinicalPearl: Formal audiological assessment is mandatory as the FIRST step in every child with speech or language delay — even if the newborn screen was normal — before any genetic, radiological, or metabolic investigation.
keyClue: 20-month-old boy with speech delay, no pointing, poor eye contact, not responding to name, and repetitive play — ASD features, but hearing must be formally assessed first.
commonExamTrap: Jumping to chromosomal/genetic testing or brain imaging without first excluding hearing loss, which is the most common and treatable cause of speech delay.
missedConcept: Hearing assessment is the first-line investigation for all children with speech or language delay, regardless of newborn screen results, because late-onset or progressive hearing loss will be missed.

clinicalStem: A mother attends the 18-month developmental review concerned that her son, who was born at term, is not yet walking independently. He can pull to stand and cruise around furniture but has never taken independent steps. He sat unsupported at 7 months, has about 10 words, points to objects he wants, and makes good eye contact. Tone appears globally reduced on examination; he is able to bear weight briefly when supported. Reflexes are present but reduced bilaterally in the lower limbs. There is no family history of neuromuscular disease. Which is the most appropriate next investigation?
options: Developmental surveillance only — reassure and review at 24 months
Creatine kinase (CK) level and thyroid function tests
MRI spine to exclude tethered cord
explanation: Independent walking is expected by 18 months; failure to walk by 18 months is a developmental red flag requiring investigation. The combination of global hypotonia, reduced reflexes, and delayed gross motor with preserved language and social skills suggests a primary neuromuscular or metabolic cause. Creatine kinase (CK) is the most important first test to screen for Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) and other muscular dystrophies causing proximal weakness and hypotonia. Thyroid function tests should also be obtained to exclude hypothyroidism as a treatable cause. These are readily available, non-invasive first-line tests.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Reassurance alone is inappropriate when walking has not been achieved at 18 months with hypotonia and reduced reflexes; this combination requires formal investigation, not watchful waiting.

CK and thyroid function tests are correct: CK screens for muscular dystrophy (DMD presents with hypotonia and delayed walking in males) and TFTs exclude hypothyroidism — both are non-invasive first-line investigations for hypotonia with motor delay.

MRI spine may be indicated if there are features of spinal cord pathology (sensory level, bladder dysfunction, spinal deformity) but is not the first-line investigation in an otherwise well toddler with generalised hypotonia and absent focal neurological signs.
NCS/EMG are second-line investigations for suspected peripheral nerve or neuromuscular junction disease and would be performed after CK and TFTs have guided the differential diagnosis.

Chromosomal microarray is appropriate if there is intellectual disability, dysmorphic features, or multi-system involvement; isolated hypotonia with motor delay without dysmorphic features requires neuromuscular workup first.
clinicalPearl: Not walking at 18 months with hypotonia and reduced reflexes is a red flag — always check CK first to exclude Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD); CK can be 50–100 times elevated before clinical weakness is obvious.
keyClue: 18-month-old boy, not yet walking, global hypotonia, reduced lower limb reflexes — neuromuscular cause must be excluded, starting with CK.
commonExamTrap: Choosing 'reassure and review at 24 months' — failure to walk at 18 months with hypotonia and reduced reflexes requires active investigation, not surveillance.
missedConcept: The developmental red flag for gross motor: not walking independently by 18 months requires investigation; CK is the critical first test to exclude DMD and other myopathies.
